1: Psalm 43 has no superscription — unlike every other psalm in Book II — confirming that Psalms 42-43 form a single composition. שָׁפְטֵנִי אֱלֹהִים וְרִיבָה רִיבִי (shāpĕṭênî ʾĕlōhîm wĕrîbāh rîbî, "Vindicate me, O God, and plead my cause") — shāpaṭ: to judge, to vindicate (in a legal dispute, the judge who decides in one's favor vindicates); rîb: a legal dispute, a lawsuit. Rîbāh rîbî: "plead my dispute/case" — the Psalmist brings God to court as his advocate against the hostile nation. מֵאִישׁ-מִרְמָה וְעַוְלָה פַלְּטֵנִי (mêʾîsh-mirmāh wĕʿawlāh pallĕṭênî, "from the deceitful and unjust man deliver me") — mirmāh: deceit, treachery; ʿawlāh: injustice, perversity — the twin character of the enemy.
2: כִּי-אַתָּה אֱלֹהֵי מָעוּזִּי (kî-ʾattāh ʾĕlōhê māʿûzzî, "For you are the God who is my stronghold") — māʿôz: fortress, stronghold (as in Ps 28:8). לָמָה זְנַחְתָּנִי (lāmāh zĕnaḥtānî, "why have you rejected me?") — zānaḥ: to reject, to cast away (the word for covenant rejection — Jer 7:29; Ps 44:9). The paradox: the God who is the stronghold has also apparently rejected. לָמָּה-קֹדֵר אֶתְהַלֵּךְ (lāmmāh-qōdêr ʾethalêk, "Why do I go about mourning?") — the same qādar (to be dark, to go about in mourning) as Ps 42:9 — the two psalms share vocabulary as well as refrain.
3: שְׁלַח-אוֹרְךָ וַאֲמִתְּךָ (shĕlaḥ-ʾôrĕkā waʾămittĕkā, "Send out your light and your truth") — the two divine guides personified: ʾôr (light) and ʾĕmet (truth/faithfulness) sent like advance envoys to lead the Psalmist back to God's holy mountain. Light and truth as the conditions for the journey to divine presence. יְנַחוּנִי (yĕnaḥûnî, "let them lead me") — nāḥāh: to guide, to lead (the shepherd-guide verb of Ps 31:3). The two divine attributes personified as guides — prefiguring the Johannine language of the Spirit of truth who leads into all truth (John 16:13).
4: וְאָבוֹאָה אֶל-מִזְבַּח אֱלֹהִים (wĕʾābôʾāh ʾel-mizbah ʾĕlōhîm, "Then I will approach the altar of God") — the mizbêaḥ (altar): the place of sacrifice and access, the point where the human and divine meet. אֶל-אֵל שִׂמְחַת גִּילִי (ʾel-ʾêl śimḥat gîlî, "to God who is my greatest joy") — śimḥat gîlî: "the joy of my exultation/dancing" — gîl (the physical, dancing joy) combined with śimḥāh (rejoicing). God himself is the content of the joy, not merely its source. וְאוֹדְךָ בְכִנּוֹר אֱלֹהִים אֱלֹהָי (wĕʾôdĕkā bĕkinnôr ʾĕlōhîm ʾĕlōhāy, "I will praise you with the harp, O God my God") — the vow of praise with the kinnôr (lyre/harp — David's instrument) once the pilgrimage is restored.
5: The third and final occurrence of the refrain: מַה-תִּשְׁתּוֹחֲחִי נַפְשִׁי — "Why are you downcast, O my soul?" — closing the literary unit of Psalms 42-43 with the identical words of 42:5 and 42:11. The three-part refrain structures the whole composition as a progressive self-counsel: each recurrence after a descent into lament is the act of self-interruption — the Psalmist refuses to end on despair. The final refrain has the same hope-turn as the previous two: יַחֲלִי לֵאלֹהִים (yaḥălî lêʾlōhîm, "Put your hope in God") — yāḥal: to wait with expectation. The last word is not despair but directed hope.
